advaita.rendering: I am the bīja (seed-cause) of all beings — the germinating ground from which every arising proceeds. No entity, moving or unmoving, could subsist once stripped of Me: stripped of Me it would be hollow, a mere śūnya (void), since I am its very ātman. The entire manifest world is, in its essence, nothing other than Myself.
advaita.bhāṣya_anchor: Śaṅkara: 'mya apakṛṣṭaṃ parityaktaṃ nirātmakaṃ śūnyaṃ hi tat syāt — ataḥ madātmakaṃ sarvam.' Stripped of Me = selfless = void; therefore everything is of-My-nature.
advaita.doctrinal_note: The verse serves Śaṅkara as a prakaraṇa-upasaṃhāra (section-summary): vibhūti enumeration closes by collapsing back into non-dual identity.

viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: I am the bīja of all beings in every condition — both the manifest and the unmanifest seed of each state they pass through. The entire moving and unmoving creation exists only because I abide within it as its ātman (inner self); nothing could be without Me as its soul-ground. The prior declaration — 'I am the ātmā dwelling in the heart of all beings' (10.20) — is here restated: sāmānādhikaraṇya (grammatical co-reference of 'I' and the world) points to My ātman-status as the very basis of being.
viśiṣṭādvaita.bhāṣya_anchor: Rāmānuja explicitly links this verse back to 10.20 ('aham ātmā guḍākeśa sarvabhūtāśayasthitaḥ') and reads 'vinā mayā' as 'without Me as their inner ātman, not as an external agent.'
viśiṣṭādvaita.doctrinal_note: Beings are real and distinct (viśiṣṭa) but their reality is entirely constituted by Bhagavān's indwelling; the verse does not collapse difference but discloses the ātman-body relation at cosmic scale.

dvaita.rendering: I am the generative seed of all beings, and nothing that exists — moving or unmoving — stands apart from My will and sustaining power. Hari alone is the independent ground; all jīvas (individual souls) and all matter are ontologically distinct from Him yet utterly dependent, unable to exist or act for even a moment without His sanction.
dvaita.bhāṣya_anchor: Madhva's brevity is deliberate: 'mayā vinā yad bhūtaṃ syāt tan nāsti' — the negative formulation underscores Hari's absolute svatantratā (independence) against which every other being is paratantra (dependent). He supports this with Mokṣadharma citation (Mahābhārata, Śāntiparva 338).
dvaita.doctrinal_note: Madhva refuses to dissolve the jīva into Brahman; the verse proves dependence, not identity. The Mokṣadharma citation anchors the claim in a second śāstric witness (Viśvarūpa, Ananta).

śuddhādvaita.rendering: What more need be said? Without Me nothing whatsoever exists — and this 'without Me' points not to bare causality but to the fact that all creation is the kārya (effect) of My dual prakṛti (nature): the cit-śakti and the māyā-śakti are both entirely Mine, so their products are entirely Mine, soaked in My presence as prasāda (grace-gift). Every atom is a modulation of Kṛṣṇa's own svarūpa (essential form).
śuddhādvaita.bhāṣya_anchor: Vallabha: 'mat-prakṛti-dvaya-kāryabhūtatayā tathā iti bhāvaḥ' — both śaktis belong to Kṛṣṇa, therefore their effects are His, not merely caused by Him from outside.
śuddhādvaita.doctrinal_note: Śuddhādvaita differs from Advaita here: the world is not māyā-illusion but real transformation of Kṛṣṇa's own being through His two śaktis; the verse is a rapturous disclosure, not a dialectical point.

bhakti.rendering: I am the bīja — the root cause of germination — for all beings. The logical ground is straightforward: no moving or unmoving entity could come to be without Me; therefore I am that foundational seed. The verse gathers all prior vibhūti illustrations into a single devotional recognition: the Lord pervades everything as its very origin.
bhakti.bhāṣya_anchor: Śrīdhara: 'yad api ca sarvabhūtānāṃ bījaṃ proharākāraṇaṃ tad aham — tatra hetuḥ: mayā vinā yat syāt tat cārācaraṃ bhūtaṃ nāsty eva.' The causal inference (tatra hetuḥ) makes the bīja-claim logical, not merely rhetorical.
bhakti.doctrinal_note: Śrīdhara's commentary is clean and free of sectarian overlay; he reads the verse as a straightforward summation, making it accessible as a bhakti-entry point for ordinary seekers.

advaita-bhakti.rendering: The bīja of all beings is caitanya (pure consciousness) conditioned by māyā as its upādhi (limiting adjunct) — and that caitanya is Myself. No moving or unmoving entity can exist apart from Me, for everything is My kārya (effect). Yet this jñāna-disclosure is simultaneously an act of grace toward Arjuna: Kṛṣṇa, who is both nirguṇa Brahman and the beloved Bhagavān, reveals the non-dual ground precisely in the register of intimate address.
advaita-bhakti.bhāṣya_anchor: Madhusūdana: 'tan māyopādhikaṃ caitanyam aham eva — yataḥ sarvaṃ mat-kāryam evety arthaḥ.' The phrase māyopādhika-caitanya is distinctively Advaita; the mat-kārya conclusion adds a devotional warmth absent from Śaṅkara's colder śūnya-formulation.
advaita-bhakti.doctrinal_note: Madhusūdana holds Advaita metaphysics and Kṛṣṇa-bhakti simultaneously: the verse is both a jñāna-upasaṃhāra (non-dual summary) and a bhakti-revelation — Kṛṣṇa's personal address to Arjuna enacts the very intimacy of the absolute.

so_what_questions: If Kṛṣṇa is the bīja of every being, does that mean every creative act — a poem, a business, a child — participates in divine generativity, or does it flatten all causes into one?

The verse closes the vibhūti catalogue: why does the enumeration of Kṛṣṇa's manifestations end with a bīja (seed) rather than a culminating peak expression? What does this rhetorical choice teach about how divine presence works?

Advaita reads 'without Me' as 'stripped of its ātman = void'; Dvaita reads it as 'without Hari's sustaining sanction = dependent.' Which reading changes how you relate to your own existence — as self-disclosure of the absolute, or as utter dependence on a sovereign?
Vallabha's mat-prakṛti-dvaya formula means even māyā is Kṛṣṇa's own śakti. Does locating evil, confusion, and finitude inside divine nature solve the problem of suffering — or intensify it?
If nothing exists 'without Me,' what does it mean to feel abandoned, to experience absence, to grieve? How do the six schools handle the phenomenology of divine withdrawal?

The verse is a summary (upasaṃhāra) of an entire chapter of self-disclosure. What is the relationship between cataloguing Kṛṣṇa's manifestations (the preceding 38 verses) and this single seed-claim? Does the list lead to the seed, or does the seed make the list possible?
Rāmānuja's ātman-body reading makes Kṛṣṇa the inner self of the cosmos. What are the ethical consequences of treating every being — enemy, animal, stranger — as a body whose inner self is divine?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you encounter a moment of creative block or existential emptiness, Śaṅkara's śūnya-formulation is a pointer: the void you feel is not the absence of Brahman but its most direct disclosure — strip away all false additions, and what remains is pure awareness. The practice is not to fill the void but to recognize it as the ātman-ground.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: In every relationship — colleague, parent, rival — Rāmānuja's ātman-body model invites you to see the other person as a body whose inner animating presence is Bhagavān. Conflict becomes harder to sustain when you recognize that the self you are fighting also houses the same divine ātman you are trying to approach through prayer.
everyday_applications.dvaita: Madhva's paratantra (dependence) framework is a daily calibration against the inflation of ego: every skill you exercise, every breath you draw, every plan that succeeds — none of it is self-caused. The Dvaita practitioner cultivates a lived awareness of being downstream of Hari's sanction, which transforms achievement from pride into gratitude.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Vallabha's mat-prakṛti-dvaya reading means your creative work — art, cooking, parenting, code — is Kṛṣṇa's own śakti expressing itself through your particular upādhi (form). This is not an excuse for passivity but an invitation to work with full energy, knowing the work is already His: the Puṣṭi-mārga practitioner offers the fruit not after the fact but as the very fabric of the doing.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Śrīdhara's clean causal inference — 'I am the bīja because nothing exists without Me' — is a simple meditation for a distracted mind: when overwhelmed by multiplicity, return to the single seed. Everything you are managing, worrying about, or celebrating is a sprout from one root. Hold the root; the sprouts will sort themselves.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusūdana's synthesis is most useful in the tension between intellectual knowledge and emotional longing: you can know intellectually that Kṛṣṇa is non-dual caitanya (consciousness) and simultaneously ache for the personal Bhagavān who addresses you by name. The verse models that very simultaneity — the abstract seed-claim delivered in the most intimate register ('he Arjuna'). You do not have to choose between understanding and loving.

self_assessment: All six bhāṣyas supplied in clean Devanagari; renderings anchored to commentator-specific formulations with genuine doctrinal divergence across the non-dual/qualified-non-dual/dualist/śakti-realist spectrum; no bhāṣya absent.
